name: Donald Trump
summary: Donald Trump's character and pattern of behavior are marked by a history of discriminatory practices, financial recklessness, and disregard for the law. He has been involved in over 4,000 lawsuits and has a record of making false promises, including his repeated pledges to release his tax returns. Trump has also demonstrated a willingness to use his power to target marginalized groups, including immigrants, Muslims, and LGBTQ individuals, and has been accused of racist and sexist behavior. His presidency was marked by numerous scandals, including his impeachment for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress, and his involvement in the January 6 Capitol attack, for which he was impeached again for incitement of insurrection. Trump's actions have consistently shown a disregard for democratic norms and institutions, and a willingness to prioritize his own interests over the well-being of the country.

entries.fact: Donald Trump countersued the U.S. government for $100 million over charges that his properties had discriminated against Black applicants and tenants, with the help of Roy Cohn.
The case was settled with a consent decree agreeing to desegregate, which the Trumps ended up in court for violating four years later.
Donald Trump filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for six of his businesses, including the Plaza Hotel in Manhattan and the casinos in Atlantic City, New Jersey.
Trump filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for the Trump Taj Mahal casino, which was financed with $675 million in junk bonds and completed for $1.1 billion.
The Plaza Hotel filed for bankruptcy protection, and a reorganization plan was approved a month later, with the banks taking control of the property.
Trump's casinos, including Trump's Castle and the Trump Plaza, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection.

Donald Trump defaulted on over $3 billion of bank loans, and the lenders seized the Plaza Hotel along with most of his other properties in a 'vast and humiliating restructuring' that allowed him to avoid personal bankruptcy.
Trump founded Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR), which assumed ownership of the Trump Plaza.
Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR) went bankrupt, leaving Trump with 10% ownership.
Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR) went bankrupt again, and Trump remained chairman until that year.
The State of New York filed a $40 million civil suit against Trump University, alleging that the company made false statements and defrauded consumers.
Donald Trump's leadership style and political agenda, often referred to as Trumpism, have reshaped the Republican Party's identity and have been characterized as racist or misogynistic.
NBC and Univision dropped the Miss Universe pageants in reaction to Trump's comments about Mexican immigrants.
The Washington Post reported that the Donald J. Trump Foundation had committed several potential legal and ethical violations, including self-dealing and tax evasion.
The New York attorney general's office ordered the Donald J. Trump Foundation to cease its fundraising activities in New York immediately.
According to a review of state and federal court files, Trump and his businesses had been involved in more than 4,000 state and federal legal actions.
Donald Trump had been involved in more than 4,000 lawsuits, liens, and other filings, often filed for nonpayment against him by employees, contractors, real estate brokers, and his own attorneys.
The New York attorney general's office filed a civil suit against the Donald J. Trump Foundation, Trump, and his adult children, seeking $2.8 million in restitution and additional penalties.
Donald Trump was impeached for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress.
Trump declared the Mar-a-Lago estate in Palm Beach, Florida, as his primary residence.
A New York state judge ordered Trump to pay $2 million to a group of charities for misusing the foundation's funds, in part to finance his presidential campaign.
Donald Trump downplayed the severity of the COVID-19 pandemic, contradicted health officials, and signed the CARES Act in response to the pandemic.

Donald Trump's administration targeted political opponents and civil society, persecuted transgender people, and carried out mass deportation of immigrants, resulting in over 550 lawsuits challenging their legality.
Donald Trump was impeached for incitement of insurrection.
Donald Trump was found liable in civil cases for sexual abuse and defamation and for business fraud.
Donald Trump was found guilty in 34 counts of falsifying business records, making him the first U.S. president convicted of a felony.
Donald Trump initiated mass layoffs of federal workers at the start of his second presidency.
Donald Trump imposed tariffs on nearly all countries at the highest level since the Great Depression and signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.

The Internal Revenue Service was investigating whether Trump had twice written off losses incurred through construction cost overruns and lagging sales of residential units in the Trump International Hotel and Tower in Chicago.
Donald Trump authorized a joint U.S./Israeli war with Iran that resulted in the killing of Iran's leader.
Donald Trump promised to release his tax returns if he ran for office.
Donald Trump again promised to release his tax returns if he ran for office.
Donald Trump announced his candidacy for the 2016 presidential election, campaigning as a rich, successful businessman and an outsider without political experience.
Donald Trump claimed Mexican immigrants were 'bringing drugs, they're bringing crime, they're rapists' in his campaign launch speech.
Donald Trump made 'explicitly racist and sexist appeals to win over white voters' during his 2016 presidential campaign.
Donald Trump did not release his tax returns, contrary to his promises and the practice of every major candidate since 1976.
Donald Trump described NATO as 'obsolete'.
Portions of Donald Trump's state filings for 1995 were leaked, showing he had declared a loss of $916 million that year, which could have let him avoid taxes for up to 18 years.
Donald Trump continued to profit from his businesses during his presidency and knew how his administration's policies affected them.
The Trump Organization pursued operational expansions in Scotland, Dubai, and the Dominican Republic, despite Donald Trump's promise to eschew 'new foreign deals'.
Donald Trump reduced the budget for renewable energy research by 40 percent and reversed Obama-era policies directed at curbing climate change.
Donald Trump withdrew from the Paris Agreement, making the U.S. the only nation to not ratify it.
Donald Trump rolled back more than 100 federal environmental regulations, including those that curbed greenhouse gas emissions, air and water pollution, and the use of toxic substances.

Trump dismantled federal regulations on health, labor, the environment, and other areas, including a bill that revoked the Obama-era regulation restricting the sale of firearms to severely mentally ill people.
Trump delayed, suspended, or reversed ninety federal regulations, often after requests by the regulated industries.
Trump vowed to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act and scaled back the act's implementation through executive orders.
Donald Trump signed six executive orders, including authorizing procedures for repealing the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act and planning for a wall along the U.S. border with Mexico.
Donald Trump signed the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, which reduced tax rates for businesses and individuals and eliminated the penalty associated with the Affordable Care Act's individual mandate.
The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 increased the federal budget deficit by almost 50 percent, to nearly $1 trillion in 2019.
Trump signed legislation to increase funding for drug treatments in response to the opioid epidemic, but was widely criticized for failing to make a concrete strategy.
The federal budget deficit increased by almost 50 percent, to nearly $1 trillion, under Donald Trump's presidency.
The U.S. national debt increased by 39 percent, reaching $27.75 trillion, and the U.S. debt-to-GDP ratio hit a post-World War II high under Donald Trump's presidency.
Trump's administration took an anti-marijuana position, revoking Obama-era policies that provided protections for states that legalized marijuana.
Trump's administration oversaw the federal government execute 13 prisoners, more than in the previous 56 years combined, ending a 17-year moratorium.
Trump's administration rolled back key components of the Obama administration's workplace protections against discrimination of LGBTQ people.
A federal judge halted Trump's attempted rollback of anti-discrimination protections for transgender patients.

Trump granted 237 requests for clemency, fewer than all presidents since 1900 with the exception of George H. W. Bush and George W. Bush, and many of them were granted to people with personal or political connections to him.
The U.S. Supreme Court allowed Donald Trump's tax returns and other records to be released to the Manhattan district attorney for a criminal investigation.
Trump resigned from SAG-AFTRA to avoid a disciplinary hearing regarding the January 6 attack, and two days later, the union permanently barred him.
Trump pledged that Roe v. Wade would be overturned 'automatically' if he were elected and given the opportunity to appoint two or three anti-abortion justices.
The FBI launched Crossfire Hurricane, an investigation into possible links between Russia and Trump's 2016 campaign.
Trump signed an executive order that denied entry to citizens from six Muslim-majority countries for four months and from Syria indefinitely.
Trump implemented a travel ban targeting predominantly Muslim countries, which was blocked by courts but later upheld by the Supreme Court in 2019.
Three U.S. intelligence agencies jointly stated with 'high confidence' that Russia interfered in the 2016 presidential election to favor Trump.
Trump disparaged courts and judges he disagreed with, often in personal terms, and questioned the judiciary's constitutional authority.
Trump dismissed Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) director James Comey, saying a few days later that he was concerned about Comey's role in the Trump-Russia investigations.
The FBI opened a second investigation into Trump's personal and business dealings with Russia after he fired Comey.
Trump replaced the initial travel ban with a presidential proclamation extending travel bans to North Koreans, Chadians, and some Venezuelan officials.
Trump was slow to appoint second-tier officials in the executive branch, saying many of the positions are unnecessary, with hundreds of sub-cabinet positions without a nominee.
The Supreme Court allowed Trump's revised travel ban to go into effect, which was ultimately upheld in 2019.
Trump's refusal to sign any spending bill unless it allocated funding for the border wall resulted in the longest-ever federal government shutdown, for 35 days from December 2018 to January 2019.
The Trump administration had a policy of family separation that separated over 4,400 children from migrant parents at the U.S.-Mexico border.
Trump mandated that illegal immigrant families be detained together unless there was a concern of risk for the child, after initially blaming Democrats for the family separation policy.
A judge ordered that the families be reunited and further separations stopped except in limited circumstances, but over 1,000 additional children were separated from their families after the order.
Trump described himself as a 'nationalist' and his foreign policy as 'America First', supporting populist, neo-nationalist, and authoritarian governments.
Trump began a trade war with China, imposing tariffs and other trade barriers to force China to end unfair trade practices and intellectual property infringement.
Trump weakened the toughest U.S. sanctions imposed after the 2014 Russian annexation of Crimea.
Mueller's final report found that Russia interfered in 2016 to favor Trump and that Trump and his campaign welcomed and encouraged the effort.
The Supreme Court upheld Trump's travel ban, which was implemented in 2017.
Trump pressured Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy to investigate Joe and Hunter Biden, in an attempt to gain an advantage in the 2020 presidential election.
Journalist E. Jean Carroll sued Trump for defamation over his denial of raping her in the 1990s.
The House voted to impeach Trump for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress.
Trump initially ignored public health warnings and calls for action from health officials within his administration regarding the COVID-19 pandemic.
Trump established the White House Coronavirus Task Force.
The Senate acquitted Trump in his first impeachment trial.
Trump encouraged right-wing protests against social-distancing policies in states with stay-at-home orders.
Trump halted funding of the World Health Organization.
Trump repeatedly pressured federal health agencies to take actions he favored, such as approving unproven treatments.
Trump reduced the number of refugees admitted to record lows, from an annual limit of 110,000 before he took office to 15,000 in 2021.
Trump took credit when Roe was overturned by Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization in 2022; all three of his Supreme Court nominees voted with the majority.
Trump sowed doubts about the election, claiming without evidence that it would be rigged and that widespread use of mail balloting would produce massive election fraud.
Trump declared victory before the results were known on the morning after the election.
Trump baselessly alleged election fraud after Joe Biden was projected the winner.
Trump and his allies filed many lawsuits challenging the election results, which were rejected by at least 86 judges in both state and federal courts for having no factual or legal basis.
Trump held a rally at which he called for the election to be overturned and urged his supporters to 'fight like hell' and 'take back our country' by marching to the Capitol.

Trump's supporters formed a mob that broke into the Capitol building, disrupting certification and causing the evacuation of Congress, resulting in over 140 police officers injured and five people dead.
The House charged Trump with incitement of insurrection for his role in the January 6 Capitol attack.
The Senate acquitted Trump in his second impeachment trial.
Donald Trump began using the term 'big lie' to refer to the 2020 election itself, after initially having his false claims about the election referred to as the 'big lie' by his critics.
The National Archives and Records Administration retrieved 15 boxes of documents Trump had taken to Mar-a-Lago after leaving the White House, some of which were classified.
Trump was found liable for sexual abuse and defamation and ordered to pay $5 million in one case and $83.3 million in the other.
FBI agents searched Mar-a-Lago for illegally held documents, including those in breach of the Espionage Act, collecting 11 sets of classified documents, some marked top secret.
New York filed a civil lawsuit against Trump accusing him of inflating the Trump Organization's value to gain an advantage with lenders and banks.
E. Jean Carroll sued Trump again for battery and further defamation.

The U.S. House committee on the January 6 attack recommended criminal charges against Trump for obstructing an official proceeding, inciting or assisting an insurrection, and conspiracy to defraud the United States.
Trump made increasingly violent and authoritarian statements during his campaign.
Trump said he would weaponize the FBI and the Justice Department against his political opponents.
Trump used harsher and more dehumanizing anti-immigrant rhetoric than during his presidency.
Trump called his political opponents 'the enemy', 'vermin', and 'fascists'.
Trump's campaign began diverting 10% of donations to his leadership PAC.
Trump's campaign paid $100 million towards his legal bills by March 2024.
A federal grand jury constituted by Special Counsel Jack Smith indicted Trump on 31 counts of 'willfully retaining national defense information' under the Espionage Act, among other charges.
A grand jury in Fulton County, Georgia, indicted Trump on 13 charges, including racketeering, for his efforts to subvert the 2020 election in the state.

Trump was convicted on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records, stemming from evidence that he booked Michael Cohen's hush-money payments to adult film actress Stormy Daniels as business expenses to cover up his alleged 2006-2007 affair with Daniels during the 2016 election.
Trump was shot in the ear in an assassination attempt at a campaign rally in Butler Township, Pennsylvania.
Donald Trump's administration asserted a constitutional right to ignore federal law, claiming a power to immunize private parties to commit otherwise illegal acts with impunity.

Trump's second presidency has been described as having fewer guardrails against conflicts of interest than his first, breaking with decades of ethical norms and raising substantial corruption concerns.
Trump became the oldest to assume the presidency and the first president with a felony conviction.
Trump issued more executive orders than any other U.S. president's first 100 days.
Trump defied judges and the courts in roughly one third of all cases against him.

Donald Trump bragged about kissing and groping women without their consent in a 2005 'hot mic' recording, saying that 'when you're a star, they let you do it. You can do anything. ... Grab 'em by the pussy.'

Trump maintained a publicly traded company, Trump Media & Technology Group, and diversified it into financial services, while also pursuing new overseas real estate deals involving state-affiliated entities.
Trump launched, promoted, and personally benefited from two cryptocurrency tokens, $Trump and $Melania, and directly benefited from his family's cryptocurrency company World Liberty Financial.
Trump threatened, signed executive actions, and ordered investigations into his political opponents, critics, and organizations aligned with the Democratic Party.

Trump's actions against civil society were described by hundreds of legal experts and political scientists as authoritarian, contributing to democratic backsliding, and negatively impacting the rule of law.
Trump's pardons and grants of clemency favored political allies and loyalists, and disproportionately pardoned the powerful, famous, well-connected, and wealthy accused of white-collar crime.

Trump granted clemency to all January 6 rioters convicted or charged on his first day in office, including those who violently attacked police, by pardoning more than 1,500 and commuting the sentences of 14.
Trump ordered agencies to stop enforcement of disfavored rules in an attempt at large-scale deregulation that legal experts described as illegal and contrary to decades of federal law.

Trump sought greater government control over private businesses and shifted away from traditional conservative free market orthodoxy, engaging in state capitalism by taking direct government equity stakes in multiple U.S. corporations.

Trump canceled and paused federal grants and made large cuts to scientific research, several of which were found by judges and the Government Accountability Office as being illegal and unconstitutional.
Trump and his administration's Make America Healthy Again agenda promoted anti-science and anti-vaccine activism, resulting in a resurgence of whooping cough and measles.
Trump relied on declaring national emergencies to justify hundreds of actions and bypass congressional approval or regulatory review.
Trump declared a national energy emergency, allowing the suspension of environmental regulations, loosening the rules for fossil fuel extraction and limiting renewable energy projects.
Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Paris Agreement on climate change during his second presidency.
Trump attributed societal problems to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives and wokeness, equating diversity with incompetence.
Trump reversed pro-diversity policies in the federal government.

Trump banned transgender people from the military, restricted or defunded gender-affirming healthcare, opposed inclusive language, censored research and education materials, targeted schools, universities, and cultural institutions accused of promoting what his government calls 'gender ideology', barred transgender athletes from sports, and required U.S. passports to state transgender people's sex assigned at birth.
Trump implemented a hiring freeze across the federal government and ordered telework of federal employees to be discontinued within 30 days.
Trump ordered a review of many career civil service positions with the intention of reclassifying them into at-will positions without job protections.
Trump initiated mass job terminations of federal employees, which were described by legal experts as unprecedented or in violation of federal law.
By late February 2025, the Trump administration had fired more than 30,000 federal employees.

Trump ordered an end to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) projects in the federal government and rescinded Lyndon B. Johnson's 1965 Executive Order 11246, which had mandated that federal contractors take affirmative action to end racial discrimination.
Trump used the Alien Enemies Act of 1798 to imprison illegal immigrants without trial at the Terrorism Confinement Center in El Salvador.

Trump and Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency largely dismantled several federal agencies, including USAID and the Department of Education, unilaterally fired several thousand staff, and reduced administrative functions to statutory minimums.
Trump targeted activists, legal immigrants, tourists, and students with visas who expressed criticism of his policies or pro-Palestinian advocacy.
Several American citizens were detained and deported.
Trump resumed the Remain in Mexico policy, designated drug cartels as terrorist groups, and ordered construction to be resumed on a border wall.
Trump revoked the parole status of immigrants who entered the U.S. under CBP One and ended humanitarian parole for Cubans, Haitians, Nicaraguans.

Trump signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act into law, making the temporary tax cuts of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act permanent and adding additional tax deductions for a total of around $4.5 trillion, mostly benefiting the highest income brackets and costing people in the lowest income bracket $1,600 per year.
Trump expanded the domestic use of the military and ordered military deployments to several Democratic Party-led cities, and threatened to expand his deployments further.
Donald Trump posed as a Trump Organization official named 'John Barron' and called journalist Jonathan Greenberg, trying to get a higher ranking on the Forbes 400 list of wealthy Americans.
The Taj Mahal casino, owned by Donald Trump, emerged from bankruptcy.
Trump's Castle and Plaza filed for bankruptcy.
Donald Trump became the leading proponent of the racist 'birther' conspiracy theory that Barack Obama, the first black U.S. president, was not born in the United States.

Donald Trump's campaign released a letter from his longtime personal physician, Harold Bornstein, stating that he would 'be the healthiest individual ever elected to the presidency', which was later revealed to have been dictated by Trump himself.
Donald Trump made over 30,573 false or misleading statements during his first presidency, according to The Washington Post's fact-checkers.
Donald Trump promoted conspiracy theories, including climate change denial, alleged Ukrainian interference in U.S. elections, and vaccines and autism.
Trump's politics and rhetoric led to the formation of a political movement known as Trumpism, which has been compared to a cult of personality.
Trump's comments and actions have been characterized as racist, including his explicit and routine disparagement of racial, religious, and ethnic minorities.
A 2005 'hot mic' recording of Donald Trump surfaced, leading to his first public apology during his 2016 presidential campaign.

Trump condemned the Unite the Right rally, but implied a moral equivalence between the white supremacist demonstrators and the counter-protesters, stating that there were 'very fine people on both sides'.
Trump reportedly referred to El Salvador, Haiti, Honduras, and African nations as 'shithole countries', remarks condemned as racist.
The House of Representatives condemned Trump for racist remarks about four minority Democratic congresswomen.

As of 2020, 26 women have publicly accused Donald Trump of sexual misconduct, including rape, kissing without consent, groping, looking under women's skirts, and walking in on naked teenage pageant contestants.

Donald Trump's rhetoric has been associated with an increased incidence of hate crimes, and he has an emboldening effect on expressing prejudicial attitudes due to his normalization of explicit racial rhetoric.

A nationwide review by ABC News identified at least 54 criminal cases, from August 2015 to April 2020, in which Donald Trump was invoked in direct connection with violence or threats of violence mostly by white men and primarily against minorities.
Twitter began to tag some of Donald Trump's tweets with fact-checks.

Federal law-enforcement officials used tear gas and other crowd control tactics to remove a largely peaceful crowd of lawful protesters from Lafayette Square, outside the White House, before Trump posed with a Bible for a photo op at the nearby St. John's Episcopal Church.

Donald Trump refused to condemn the white supremacist Proud Boys during a 2020 presidential debate, and his comment, 'Proud Boys, stand back and stand by', was said to have led to increased recruitment for the pro-Trump group.
Donald Trump tried and failed to overturn the election with voter fraud claims.
Donald Trump was banned from Twitter after the January 6 attack.
Trump's 2024 campaign made extensive use of dehumanizing language and racial stereotypes.
Trump imposed tariffs on most countries, including large tariffs on major trading partners China, Canada, and Mexico.
Trump selected cabinet members with personal loyalty to him, with the 'focus on loyalty over subject-matter expertise'.
Trump and Vice President Vance berated Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy in a highly contentious televised meeting.
Trump ordered the bombing of Iranian nuclear sites.
Trump pardoned former Honduran president Juan Orlando Hernández, who had been extradited to the U.S. in 2022 and sentenced to 45 years in prison for drug trafficking.
Trump's aggressive and hardline deportation campaign led to large nationwide protests and violent confrontations with migrants and protesters.
The Supreme Court ruled the tariffs Trump imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to be illegal.
Trump authorized joint U.S./Israeli air strikes on Iran.
An investigation found that Donald Trump's charity, the Trump Foundation, spent other people's money.
The Trump Foundation was ordered to stop fundraising by the New York attorney general's office.
Donald Trump agreed to pay $25 million in a settlement related to Trump University.
Donald Trump announced that he would dissolve his charitable foundation after mounting complaints.
Trump issued an executive order scaling back parts of Obamacare
Donald Trump issued a travel ban that caused chaos and protests at US airports, affecting immigration and refugees.
Three of Trump's agents seized his medical records in a raid on his physician Harold Bornstein's office.
Trump rolled back an Obama regulation that made it harder for people with mental illnesses to purchase guns
Trump signed a new travel ban directive, which was later halted by a Hawaii judge before it could go into effect.
The Supreme Court partly reinstated Trump's travel ban, allowing it to go into effect for some individuals.
Trump said he planned to 'let Obamacare fail'
Trump cut funding for Obamacare enrollment programs by 40%, and also cut the enrollment period in half
Trump extended his travel ban to include blocks on North Korea, Venezuela, and Chad.
Trump signed a health care executive order that allowed small businesses and associations to band together to buy health insurance plans that might not meet Obamacare's standards
The Trump administration announced that it would not defend the Affordable Care Act in court, effectively abandoning its duty to defend the law
Trump's Federal Communications Commission (FCC) voted to repeal net neutrality rules, which could harm consumers and small businesses
The Trump Foundation agreed to dissolve, accused of a 'shocking pattern of illegality'.
Trump proposed cuts to climate and clean-energy programs
President Donald Trump was ordered to pay $2 million to a collection of non-profits as part of a civil lawsuit.
The Trump administration was accused of sidelining researchers and their work on climate change and other scientific issues
Trump's fossil fuel agenda was pushed back against by federal judges
Donald Trump was banned from Facebook and Instagram after the January 6 attack.
Donald Trump launched social media platform Truth Social.
Twitter reinstated Donald Trump's account.
The two-year ban at Meta Platforms lapsed, allowing Donald Trump to return to Facebook and Instagram.
Donald Trump repeatedly voiced support for outlawing political dissent and criticism.
Donald Trump said that reporters should be prosecuted for not divulging confidential sources and media companies should possibly lose their broadcast licenses for unfavorable coverage of him.
Despite promising to release files relating to Jeffrey Epstein during the 2024 campaign, the Trump administration did not release the files, sparking media attention and public pressure.
Some of the files related to Jeffrey Epstein were released, mostly heavily redacted, after media attention and public pressure mounted.

Roughly three million additional pages of files related to Jeffrey Epstein were released, including approximately 38,000 references to Trump, his wife, Mar-a-Lago, and other Trump-related terms, with some references including 'salacious information' and uncorroborated statements about him from witnesses in transcripts made over several decades.
The Supreme Court allowed Trump's latest travel ban to go into full effect.
The Supreme Court upheld Trump's travel ban in a 5-4 decision.
Trump signed a bill to temporarily reopen the government after the longest shutdown in US history, which was caused in part by his demand for border wall funding.
Trump declared a national emergency to build a border wall, setting up a massive legal fight.
Trump used words like 'invasion' and 'killer' at rallies more than 500 times since 2017, according to a USA Today analysis.
Trump cut refugee admissions to the US to a record low.
E. Jean Carroll, who accused Trump of rape, sued him for defamation
Trump virtually cut off refugees as he unleashed a tirade on immigrants.
Trump proclaimed that 'the coal industry is back', despite evidence to the contrary
The Trump administration had sabotaged the Affordable Care Act in six different ways
The Trump administration had rolled back over 100 environmental rules
The Trump administration had built a national debt so big that it would weigh down the economy for years
The Trump administration carried out its 13th and final execution, marking the end of a series of federal executions.
The Trump administration had rolled back more than 100 environmental rules, according to a New York Times analysis
Trump had broken several notable promises, including his promise to bring back coal jobs
Agents at Trump's Mar-a-Lago seized 11 sets of classified documents, court filing shows
New York attorney general filed civil fraud lawsuit against Trump, some of his children, and his business
Writer who accused Trump of rape to file new defamation lawsuit
Trump put national secrets at risk, prosecutors say in historic indictment
Trump pleaded not guilty to 37 federal criminal counts in Mar-a-Lago case
Donald Trump and allies were indicted in Georgia over bid to reverse 2020 election loss
An appeals court upheld E. Jean Carroll's $83.3M defamation judgment against Trump
Trump fraud trial penalty will exceed $450 million
Trump was convicted on all counts to become America's first felon president
A federal judge dismissed Trump classified documents case over concerns with prosecutor's appointment
With D.C. case dismissed, Trump was no longer under federal indictment
An appeals court upheld a $5 million award in a sexual abuse verdict against President-elect Trump
Trump received an unconditional discharge sentence in the hush money case
A divided court eliminated Trump's half-billion-dollar fine in fraud case
A judge dismissed Georgia election interference case against Trump
Donald Trump raised birther questions about President Obama, suggesting he was not born in the United States.
Donald Trump entertained conspiracy theories, including the idea that President Obama was not born in the United States.
Donald Trump made false allegations against President Obama, including accusing him of treason.
Donald Trump made unprecedented falsehoods for a modern presidential candidate, according to the Los Angeles Times.
Donald Trump made false claims about the size of his inaugural crowd, with metrics showing it was not the largest.
Trump signed executive order directing US withdrawal from the Paris climate agreement
Trump signed sweeping actions on immigration and border security
Trump ordered federal workers to report on colleagues over diversity, equity, and inclusion crackdown
Trump proposed a plan to 'clean out' Gaza and suggested that Jordan and Egypt should accept more Palestinian refugees
Trump's energy emergency declaration is seen as a gift to fossil fuel firms
Trump attacked diversity, and a racist undercurrent surfaced
Trump's executive actions curbing transgender rights focused on 'gender ideology'
Trump expressed indifference or hostility towards European allies, raising alarm
The Trump administration continued to hollow out the federal workforce, firing more than 30,000 employees.
Trump had a tense meeting with Ukrainian President Zelensky in the Oval Office
Trump's clash with Zelensky marked a defining turn away from US defense of democracies
Trump's tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China took effect
El Salvador's mega-prison held Venezuelans deported from the US as part of Trump's immigration crackdown
Trump's science policies posed long-term risks, according to economists
A federal judge deemed Trump's termination of NIH grants illegal
Trump embraced state capitalism
Trump imposed new tariffs with no precedent in the modern era
Staggering US tariffs began as Trump widened the trade war
Trump illegally froze 1,800 NIH medical research grants, according to Congress' watchdog
Trump tightened his grip on the economy, taking a page from China
Trump wants to command bosses like Xi does, but is failing
Trump used 10 emergency declarations to justify hundreds of actions
Trump's escalating attacks on vaccines shocked public health leaders
Anti-science bills hit statehouses, stripping away public health protections built over a century
A new passport rule sent a blunt and sweeping message to trans Americans
Donald Trump's lies were analyzed in an article by Politico Magazine, which explored the effects of his falsehoods on the public.
Donald Trump's frequency of lying increased over time, with an analysis by The New Yorker showing he was lying more and doing it on purpose.
Donald Trump showed a pattern of not apologizing for his actions, with some critics arguing that this behavior was harmful to his relationships and reputation.
Donald Trump had made over 5,000 false statements as president, according to an analysis by the Toronto Star.
The concept of the 'Trump Defense' emerged, where individuals invoked the president's name to justify violent or threatening behavior.
Donald Trump's rhetoric was linked to 54 cases of violence, threats, or alleged assaults, according to an analysis by ABC News.
Donald Trump promoted an unproven coronavirus treatment, hydroxychloroquine, which led to shortages for patients who needed the drug.
Donald Trump's failure to address the COVID-19 pandemic was attributed to his own actions and decisions, according to an investigation by The New York Times.
Donald Trump claimed that mail-in voting was rigged, despite a lack of evidence, according to an AP Fact Check.
Donald Trump refused to condemn white supremacists and militia members during a presidential debate.
Donald Trump had a long history of promoting conspiracy theories, including the idea that President Obama was not born in the United States.
Rioters who stormed the US Capitol cited Donald Trump's words as inspiration for their actions, according to accounts reported by The Washington Post.
Donald Trump made 30,573 false or misleading claims during his four years as president, according to an analysis by The Washington Post.
Research suggests that Donald Trump's most loyal supporters exhibit personality characteristics of a personality cult.
Donald Trump escalated his rhetoric against political dissent and criticism, calling it 'totally illegal'.
President Trump claimed he 'brought back free speech', but his first 100 days in office told a different story, with many instances of him attacking the media and limiting free speech.
Trump's Venezuelan boat violated international law, according to experts
The Trump administration joined Republicans' campaign to police speech, sparking fears of a 'McCarthy era' and attacks on free speech.
Trump announced that 8 European countries would face a 10% tariff for opposing US control of Greenland
Trump said he had a framework for a Greenland deal as NATO considered US sovereignty over bases
Donald Trump said Ukraine must reach a deal with Russia fast before trilateral talks.
Donald Trump was a defendant in the court case Brutus v. Trump.
Donald Trump's company, Richmond Capital Group LLC, was involved in a court case, People v. Richmond Capital Group LLC, which was documented on CourtListener.
A court ruled on a case, Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump Revisions, on February 20, 2026.
Donald Trump was involved in a court case, Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump, which was documented on CourtListener.
The Supreme Court ruled that Trump's sweeping emergency tariffs were illegal
A court ruled on a case, Lesly Miot v. Trump, on February 23, 2026.
A court ruled on a case, American Federation of Government Employees, Afl-Cio v. Trump, on February 26, 2026.
Trump's attack on Iran was seen as the ultimate war of choice
A court case, Fritz Emmanuel Lesly Miot v. Donald Trump, was documented.
Donald Trump was a defendant in the court case State of Oregon v. Trump.
Donald Trump was a defendant in the court case Schiff v. Trump.
Trump pressured Colorado Gov. Polis to release Tina Peters, a former county clerk convicted in a scheme to find proof of fraud in the 2020 presidential election
The DOJ released Epstein files containing sexual assault allegations against Trump, including notes from FBI interviews with a woman who alleges she was assaulted by Trump in the 1980s.
Trump ruled out talks with Iran absent its 'unconditional surrender' as Israel strikes Lebanon
Multiple states sued over Trump's new global tariffs imposed after his Supreme Court loss

President Trump's urging of pregnant women to avoid taking Tylenol due to an unfounded claim that it could be linked to autism in children resulted in a notable drop in orders for the medication among pregnancy-related emergency department visits.
